edges.description: AV nodal suppression increases conduction delay across successive beats.
Ischemic atrioventricular nodal depression produces progressive conduction delay across successive beats.
Progressive AV nodal conduction delay culminates in a nonconducted atrial impulse.
Intermittent dropped beats can reduce ventricular rate and contribute to bradycardic symptoms.

edges.source: Atropine for Acute Reversible AV Nodal Block
Pacemaker Therapy for Symptomatic or Hemodynamically Unstable Wenckebach
Pacemaker Therapy for Symptomatic or Hemodynamically Unstable Wenckebach

nodes.description: Atropine may be used in acute vagally mediated or drug-associated AV nodal bradyarrhythmia while removing the reversible trigger; this is not evidence for chronic treatment of asymptomatic nodal Wenckebach.
nodes.id: Atropine for Acute Reversible AV Nodal Block

evidence.reference_title: Repeated complete atrioventricular block during remifentanil administration in a pediatric patient with brain tumor and acute hydrocephalus: a case report.
evidence.snippet: Remifentanil was immediately discontinued, and we administered atropine sulfate. Complete atrioventricular block was restored to sinus rhythm.

nodes.description: Intermittent dropped ventricular beats can produce a slow ventricular rate.

evidence.reference_title: Repeated complete atrioventricular block during remifentanil administration in a pediatric patient with brain tumor and acute hydrocephalus: a case report.
evidence.snippet: Remifentanil is known to cause sinus bradycardia, however, because it has a direct negative chronotropic effect on the cardiac conduction system and there is an indirect negative chronotropic effect via the parasympathetic nervous system.

nodes.description: Acute inferior myocardial infarction can produce ischemic atrioventricular nodal conduction depression with Wenckebach periodicity, converging on the progressive PR-prolongation cascade.

evidence.reference_title: Alternating Wenckebach periods in acute inferior myocardial infarction: clinical, electrocardiographic, and therapeutic characterization.
evidence.snippet: We report on twelve patients with alternating Wenckebach periods (AWP) occurring during an acute inferior myocardial infarction (AIMI).

nodes.description: The Wenckebach cycle culminates in a nonconducted P wave and dropped ventricular beat, lowering the effective ventricular rate.

evidence.reference_title: The incidence of typical and atypical A-V Wenckebach periodicity.
evidence.snippet: the pause produced by the nonconducted P-wave is less than two P-P intervals.

nodes.description: Pacemaker therapy can be used for symptomatic or hemodynamically unstable Wenckebach presentations, especially in acute ischemic or non-benign contexts; this is conditional and distinct from asymptomatic nodal Wenckebach, where pacing may be inappropriate.
nodes.id: Pacemaker Therapy for Symptomatic or Hemodynamically Unstable Wenckebach

evidence.reference_title: Alternating Wenckebach periods in acute inferior myocardial infarction: clinical, electrocardiographic, and therapeutic characterization.
evidence.snippet: Pacemaker therapy was initiated prophylactically in two patients, because of syncope in six, because of hemodynamic deterioration in two, and for syncope and hemodynamic deterioration in two.

nodes.description: In Wenckebach periodicity, successive conducted beats show progressive PR interval lengthening before a nonconducted atrial impulse.

evidence.reference_title: The incidence of typical and atypical A-V Wenckebach periodicity.
evidence.snippet: The classic pattern of the typical WP's consists of (1) progressive lengthening of the P-R intervals with the largest increment occuring in the second conducted beat

nodes.description: Symptomatic episodes may occur when bradycardia causes transient cerebral hypoperfusion.

evidence.snippet: In general, patients with second degree AV block may have no symptoms or may experience symptoms like syncope and lightheadedness.

nodes.description: Vagal or parasympathetic surges can slow sinus rate and suppress atrioventricular nodal conduction, producing a generally nodal form of Wenckebach physiology.

evidence.reference_title: Mobitz type II second-degree atrioventricular block: a commonly overdiagnosed and misinterpreted arrhythmia.
evidence.snippet: The diagnosis of Mobitz type II block AVB requires a stable sinus rate, which is an important criterion because a vagal surge (generally benign) can cause simultaneous sinus slowing and AV nodal block, which can resemble Mobitz type II AVB.

evidence.reference_title: Repeated complete atrioventricular block during remifentanil administration in a pediatric patient with brain tumor and acute hydrocephalus: a case report.
evidence.snippet: Remifentanil is known to cause sinus bradycardia, however, because it has a direct negative chronotropic effect on the cardiac conduction system and there is an indirect negative chronotropic effect via the parasympathetic nervous system.
